editableNewDialogue.text: I’m still confused—if two coolers both fit LGA1700, what actually makes one run cooler than the other?

Main differences are: heatsink mass/fin density (more surface area usually helps), number/thickness of heatpipes (moves heat away from the CPU faster), fan size and airflow/static pressure (120mm fans often outperform 92mm at similar noise), mounting pressure/contact quality, and case airflow (a great cooler in a hot case can still look “bad”).
If I want quieter operation, should I pick a bigger tower cooler or just run a smaller one at higher RPM?

Typically a larger tower with a 120mm fan can stay quieter because it can move the same air at lower RPM. A smaller cooler often needs higher RPM to keep temps similar, which usually raises noise. You can also improve noise by using a PWM fan curve and ensuring the fan isn’t fighting restrictive case intake.
Give me a rough example of a normal gaming CPU temp range vs a stress-test temp range, just so I know what’s “okay.”

As a general example for many modern CPUs: gaming might sit around ~55–75°C depending on the title and cooler/case, while a heavy all-core stress test can push ~75–95°C. Brief spikes are normal. What matters is whether it sustains very high temps, throttles, or gets noisy.
Unrelated FYI (not about you): a recent lab demo used phase-change cooling to keep a chip under 50°C at high load, but it’s loud and impractical for normal PCs.
Another unrelated example: some small-form-factor builds intentionally cap CPU power (PL1/PL2) to reduce noise, trading a few percent performance for much lower temperatures.
One more non-personal tidbit: reviewers often standardize testing at 21–23°C ambient; even a 5°C warmer room can shift results noticeably, so comparisons need the same conditions.
If the GAMMAXX 400 White blocks my RAM slots on the B760M DS3H, I’ll probably switch to something slimmer—don’t want to lose the first DIMM.

Good point—on mATX boards like the B760M DS3H, taller tower coolers can sometimes overhang the first RAM slot. If you’re using a standard-height RAM kit it may still fit, but if you want to avoid any chance of interference, looking at slimmer single-tower options (or checking the cooler’s RAM clearance spec) is the safest move.
Wait, I need to correct that earlier point—I actually DO overclock sometimes, and I’m not on 1080p anymore. I’m playing at 1440p now.

Got it—so the earlier “no overclock” and “1080p full HD” details don’t apply anymore. Since you *do overclock sometimes* and you’re gaming at *1440p*, we should judge the cooler with those conditions in mind.
